title: Life Beyond Sixty: What People Don't Say
subtitle: A short reading on what the research shows about wellbeing in the sixties and beyond
excerpt: A short reading on what the research actually shows about life satisfaction, cognitive change, and social connection in the sixties and beyond. The cultural narrative about this decade is largely wrong, and the evidence says so.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Draws on longitudinal wellbeing research including Blanchflower and Oswald's U-curve findings, Carstensen's socioemotional selectivity theory, and cognitive ageing research.

practice.whenToUse: Use when the cultural narrative about getting older is louder than the actual evidence. Good at any point in this decade when a different frame would help.

content.text: Most of what the culture says about ageing in the sixties and beyond is wrong. The research tells a different story.

Life satisfaction follows a U-curve in most large studies: high in young adulthood, lowest around the mid-forties to mid-fifties, and rising again from the late fifties onward. By the early sixties, reported wellbeing typically exceeds that of the thirties. This holds across different countries, income levels, and ways of measuring it.

This is not wishful thinking or rationalisation. The pattern emerges even when researchers control for selection effects (the possibility that happier people simply live longer). It is a real feature of how people experience this phase.

Emotional regulation tends to improve with age. Reactivity to minor frustrations and social conflict decreases. The ability to see a situation from multiple angles before responding gets more practised. There is also a shift in what counts: research shows that as people get older, they consistently prioritise depth over breadth in relationships and activities. The social circle narrows, and satisfaction with it increases.
Processing speed does slow with age. The speed at which new information is taken in and tasks are switched between peaks in the mid-twenties and declines gradually. This is real and worth knowing.

What is less often said: the accumulated store of knowledge, vocabulary, pattern recognition, and judgement does not decline the same way. It continues to build into the sixties and often beyond. The trade is real but partial: slower processing, deeper resource base.
What the cultural narrative gets wrong

The dominant cultural story about this decade is loss: loss of youth, speed, visibility, relevance. This is accurate about some things and silent about others. It does not account for the rise in reported wellbeing, the shift in relationship quality, or the practical freedom that arrives when caregiving eases and the pressure to perform a public role decreases.
The sixties are not simply a diminished version of the forties. They are a different configuration, with different costs and different capacities.
Original to homemade.education. It draws on studies of how wellbeing and thinking change in later life.
